coredata.dc:title: A new strategy to design colorful ratiometric probes and its application to fluorescent detection of Hg(II)
coredata.dc:creator: Ghasemi, Forough
Hormozi-Nezhad, M. Reza
Mahmoudi, Morteza
coredata.dc:description: A new strategy has been proposed to expand color-tunability of ratiometric fluorescent probes. It was shown that the combination of blue emissive color (as an internal standard) and yellow emissive color (as a probe) is an efficient way to create an extensive color range in ratiometric probes. However, due to the nature of the interaction between the analyte and the probe in terms of fluorescence quenching, occurance of the redshift in the emission is the major provision of such a probe. Our developed ratiometric fluorescence probe consists of blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs) and thioglycolic acid (TGA)-capped yellow emissive cadmium telluride (CdTe) quantum dots (YQDs). The ratiometric probe exhibits dual-emissions which are centered at 443 and 560 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 360 nm. With the exposure of Hg(II) ions (as an example) to the probe, the fluorescence of YQDs is selectively quenched and redshifted. The emission of probe displays continuous color changes from strong green into light green, yellow-green, yellow, orange, pink, purple, weak blue, and dark blue. The fluorescent ratiometric probe demonstrates a broad dynamic linear range from 10 nmol L−1 to 1.4 μmol L−1 with a detection limit as low as 4.6 nmol L−1. In addition, the wide-color-varying probe shows an excellent capacity to determine Hg(II) ions in environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Color-tunable ratiometric nanosensor
Quantum dots
Carbon dots
Hg(II) detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of blue emissive carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the proposed strategy to achieve a full spectrum of colors by the use of blue CDs and yellow QDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Interaction of Hg(II) ions and CdTe QDs leading the formation of (a) redshift in the emission spectra and (b) emission quenching of the CdTe QDs.
The fluorescence spectra of the mixing BCDs/YQDs and BCDs/RQDs. Inset shows corresponding fluorescence images taken under 365 nm UV lamp.

The fluorescence images of (a) the ratiometric probe (YQDs/BCDs), (b) the pure YQDs, and (c) ratiometric probe (RQDs/BCDs) upon the exposure to various concentrations of Hg(II). All images were taken under 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe upon exposure to different concentrations of Hg(II) ions, (b) linear relationship between signal of (I0-I)/I0, (I = I580/I443), and Hg(II) concentration in the range of 0.01 μmol L−1 to 1.4 μmol L−1.
(a) Fluorescence responses of the ratiometric probe towards various metal ions, and (b) corresponding fluorescence images of the probe solutions under 365 nm UV lamp.
Determination of Hg(II) ions in water samples.

head.ce:title: A new strategy to design colorful ratiometric probes and its application to fluorescent detection of Hg(II)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology and Nanotechnology Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran 13169-43551, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanotechnology and Nanotechnology Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Anesthesiology, Brigham and Women’s Hospital, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA 02115, United States

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, 11155-9516, Iran.
Corresponding author at: Department of Nanotechnology and Nanotechnology Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran 13169–43551, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy has been proposed to expand color-tunability of ratiometric fluorescent probes. It was shown that the combination of blue emissive color (as an internal standard) and yellow emissive color (as a probe) is an efficient way to create an extensive color range in ratiometric probes. However, due to the nature of the interaction between the analyte and the probe in terms of fluorescence quenching, occurance of the redshift in the emission is the major provision of such a probe. Our developed ratiometric fluorescence probe consists of blue emissive carbon dots (BCDs) and thioglycolic acid (TGA)-capped yellow emissive cadmium telluride (CdTe) quantum dots (YQDs). The ratiometric probe exhibits dual-emissions which are centered at 443 and 560 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 360 nm. With the exposure of Hg(II) ions (as an example) to the probe, the fluorescence of YQDs is selectively quenched and redshifted. The emission of probe displays continuous color changes from strong green into light green, yellow-green, yellow, orange, pink, purple, weak blue, and dark blue. The fluorescent ratiometric probe demonstrates a broad dynamic linear range from 10 nmol L−1 to 1.4 μmol L−1 with a detection limit as low as 4.6 nmol L−1. In addition, the wide-color-varying probe shows an excellent capacity to determine Hg(II) ions in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Colloidal quantum dots (QDs) have attracted extensive attention in chemical and biological application [1–4]. Photophysical properties (e.g., high quantum efficiency, size-dependent fluorescence, broad excitation spectra, high photobleaching threshold, and narrow/symmetric band emission) make QDs work much better for sensing applications compared to other fluorophores as luminescent molecular probes [5,6]. To date, QDs have been used to detect various important analytes based on the quenching or enhancement of fluorescence intensity in a single wavelength [7–9]. However, intensity of fluorescent signal could be easily disturbed by various factors, such as background fluorescence, the concentration change of probe, fluctuations in excitation source, environmental conditions of the complex samples, and temperature [10,11].

To overcome these undesirable effects, a ratiometric sensor has been developed by adding a second fluorophore [12–23]. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities is independent to the concentration of probe, excitation source fluctuations, and background fluorescence. Furthermore, our eyes are more sensitive to fluorescence color change rather than fluorescence intensity. Thus, ratiometric fluorescent probe allows for impressive quantitative and qualitative measurements.

Ratiometric fluorescent probes can be established by the combination of two fluorophores: the analyte-dependent fluorophore and an internal standard. Due to their inertness behavior, carbon nanodots (CDs) are right candidates for internal standard in the design of a ratiometric fluorescent probe [13,14,18,20]. CDs are new class of carbon nanomaterials that are strongly fluorescent, highly resistant to photobleaching, water-soluble, biocompatibile, chemically inert, and can be easily synthesized at low cost [24,25].

One of the outstanding advantageous of the ratiometric probes is to achieve a range of emission colors rather than reduction or increase in the intensity of a color. There are three typical situations on the range of color variation when two fluorophores are mixed in ratio of 1:1 to form a ratiometric probe [23]: (i) the mixture of red and blue leads to color variation from purple to red or from purple to blue; (ii) the combination of red and green leads to color variation from yellow to red or from yellow to green; And (iii) the mixture of green and blue leads to color changes from cyan to green or from cyan to blue. Zhou et al. [23] proposed that by the use of red and cyan in ratio of 5:1, one can achieve color variations from red to cyan which is wider color change compared to the predetermined mixtures. The gradual quenching of red fluorescence QDs produced a wide color change from red to cyan to the presence of arsenic ions. Although introduced color combination made an extension in observable color range, it does not cover the entire spectrum of color. Besides, this mixture (i.e., red and cyan) is just well-suited for the probe in which only quenching occurs in the QDs.

In the present study, we develope a novel strategy to expand color-tunability in ratiometric fluorescent probes by mixing blue emissive CDs (BCDs) as internal standard and yellow emissive QDs (YQDs) as the sensing fluorophore. We believe the combination of BCDs and YQDs will be a promising approach to create an extensive color range in ratiometric probes. However, due to the nature of the interaction between the analyte and the probe in terms of fluorescence quenching, occurance of the redshift in the emission is the major provision of such a probe. As a result of redshift in emission spectra, a color change from yellow to orange (or even red in case of intense redshift) can be observed in QDs. The occurrence of orange or red color causes larger number of color combinations so that we can have a full spectrum of colors from green to blue (see Scheme 1 ).

Mercury is one of the most toxic heavy elements with bioaccumulative properties triggering severe health and environmental problems [21]. Solvated mercuric ion (Hg(II)) causes damage to many human organs such as brain, heart, kidney, stomach, and intestine [26–29]. Therfore, development of a simple, rapid, sensitive, and selective detection method for trace amount of Hg(II) ions has been the subject of strong interest [30–36]. To this end, various fluorescente turn-on, turn-off [37–57] and some ratiometric type methods have been developed to detect Hg(II) ions [17,21,22,58–60]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report of full spectrum naked-eye detection by the use ratiometric fluorescent probe. Inspired by predetermined idea, we fabricate a fluorescent ratiometric probe composed of BCDs and YQDs for selective and sensitive determination of Hg(II) ions.

Tellurium powder (Te), thioglycolic acid (TGA), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), and cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2H2O) were purchased from Sigma. Citric acid, urea, mercury(II) chloride (HgCl2), and other ions (in chloride form) were obtained from Merck. Deionized water, with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ, was used in all the experiments.

The fluorescence spectroscopy was performed using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Varian) with the use of 0.2 × 1-cm quartz cell. Absorbance spectra were obtained with a Lambda25 spectrophotometer from Perkin Elmer and the use of 1.0 cm glass cell. All the spectra were recorded at room temperature. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded with a Veeco CP Research instrument equipped with Si cantilever under ambient conditions. Size distributions of the particles were collected using Zetasizer Viscotec 802 at ambient temperature. All images were recorded in 96 well fluorescence microplate by a phone camera (Samsung Galaxy S7).

TGA-capped CdTe QDs were prepared based on the procedure described in our previous article [2]. Typically, 2.87 mmol of TGA was added to 10 mL of 0.290 mol L−1 CdCl2 solution and the pH was adjusted to 9.0 with NaOH solution. The solution was then stirred under argon bubbling for 5 min. Te powder (0.76 mmol) was separately added to 75 mL of NaBH4 solution (64 mmol L−1) under argon in a three neck round bottom flask at 50 °C. After 20 min, 10 mL of pH-adjusted CdCl2 solution was added and temperature was increased to 150 °C. The resulting TGA-capped CdTe QDs with different emission were taken out at different reaction times and allowed to cool in ice bath. As-prepared yellow and red CdTe QDs (YQDs and RQDs) exhibited an emission peak centered at 560 and 618 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue emissive carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Water soluble BCDs were prepared using a hydrothermal synthetic route described previously with slight modification [61]. Briefly, 3.0 g citric acid and 3.0 g urea were dissolved into 10 mL of deionized water, and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h.

Aqueous solution of BCDs was dropwise added into diluted YQDs solution and thoroughly stirred. The concentration of YQDs was 2.5 μmol L−1 (calculated by empirical mathematical functions reported by Yu et al. [62]), and the ratio of emission intensity at 560 and 443 nm was defined as 4:1. In all experiments, the fluorescence spectra were recorded from 380 to 680 nm at excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and the band-slits of excitation and emission were set as 5.0 nm.

To detect Hg(II), 200 μL of ratiometric probe and 100 μL of phosphate buffer (pH 8.0, 100 mmol L−1), were transferred into a 1.0 mL microcentrifuge tube and were thoroughly mixed. Then, 200 μL of Hg(II) solution (containing different concentration) was added into the mixture, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after 4 min incubation at room temperature. Fluorescence intensity of YQDs at 580 nm and BCDs at 443 nm were used for quantitative analysis of Hg(II) ions.

To investigate the interference of other metal ions, fluorescent responses of the ratiometric probe were measured by the same procedure mentioned above. Selected concentrations for interference mesurments were 2.5 μmol L−1 of Hg(II) ions, 250 μmol L−1 of K+, Na+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Mg2+ ions, and 25 μmol L−1 of Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+ ions.

In order to investigate the applicability of the proposed technique in real samples, detection of Hg(II) was carried out in Tap water, Zayandeh river water, Chalus river water, Sea of Oman water and Persian Gulf water. Water samples were spiked with Hg(II) ions, filtered using filter paper (11 μm), diluted (Tap water and Zayanderud water; 0 times, Chalus river water; 5 times, Sea of Oman water and Persian Gulf water; 30 times), and analyzed in triplicate using the method mentioned above.

The main aim of this research was to introduce a new ratiometric probe for analytes which cause redshift in the probe. To do this, we chose Hg(II) as an example because, besides quenching issue, it leads to redshift in QDs (see Fig. S1). HgTe with obviously lower Ksp value can displace the CdTe on the surface of CdTe QDs via cation-exchange reaction (the Ksp of HgTe is 20 times lower than that of CdTe) [63]. The bandgap of HgTe is smaller than that of CdTe, therefore the redshift of the emission spectra takes place due to the cation-exchange [63,64]. Hg(II) can also compete with the thiol ligands on the surface of QDs leading to an emission quenching (see Scheme 2 ) [64].

TGA-capped CdTe QDs exposing yellow emission were directly synthesized in aqueous solution. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared YQDs are demonstrated in Fig. S2. YQDs possess an absorption maximum of the first electronic transition at 514 nm and a narrow emission band was centered at 560 nm. The approximate particle size was determined 2.7 nm from the first absorption maximum and the use of empirical formula [62]. The results of DLS measurements are shown in Fig. S3, demonstrating YQDs are well-dispersed. BCDs were synthesized using citric acid and urea as starting materials, exhibiting an emission peak at 443 nm (see Fig. S2b). As shown in Fig. S4, the AFM images clearly confirmed the formation of CDs with an average size of 5.6 nm.

BCDs were mixed with YQDs, and the ratio of fluorescent intensity was adjusted to 1:4 (BCDs:YQDs) to form green emission color (see Fig. 1 ). Then, the effective parameters on sensor response including pH and interaction time were investigated. To evaluate the effect of time on the probe response, the emission spectra of ratiometric probe was monitored over time. As shown in Fig. S5a, interaction of Hg(II) ions and YQDs was almost completed within 4 min. Therefore, the entire measurements were performed 4 min after adding analyte. Then, the effect of pH on the quenching process was investigated. It has been proposed that the capping agents (TGA) are removed from the surface of the TGA-capped QDs at low pH values and cause instability of QDs [65]. Therfore, the influence of pH was investigated in values above 5.0. The quenching effect of Hg(II) ions were more in alkaline media because of electrostatic interaction between Hg(II) ions and negative charged QDs. As shown in Fig. S5b, the maximum fluorescence quenching was observed at pH 8.0. Thus, pH 8.0 was chosen as the optimal pH for further experiments. It is worth mentioning that phosphate buffer forms insoluble salts with some bivalent metals and precipitate [66]. Thus, we did all experiments in phosphate buffer to eliminate the probable interference of other cationic ions.

Under the optimum conditions, different concentration of Hg(II) ions were exposed to ratiometric probe. The emission of probe was transformed from strong green into light green, yellow-green, yellow, orange, pink, purple, weak blue, and dark blue (respectively; see Fig. 2 a). Due to the detected redshift, the emission color of YQDs changed from yellow to orange. As expected, the occurrence of orange color in different intensities and its combination with blue color of reference resulted in orange, pink, and purple colors. In fact, wise selection of YQDs as probe (and BCDs as reference) led to a wide range of color variations which can be clearly observed by the naked eye.

To show the advantage of the ratiometric probe over single fluorescence quenching, we did the same experiments on pure YQDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, various concentrations of Hg(II) ions are hardly distinct to each other in comparison to ratiometric probe. To indicate the importance of color selection, red QDs (RQDs) were combined with BCDs at the same concentrations with YQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 2c, the range of colors are limited since the redshift in fluorescent spectra of RQDs makes no change in the emission color of RQDs. Therefore, to take advantage of redshift, one should use YQDs in ratiometric probe.

Under the optimum conditions, the response of ratiometric probe to different concentration of Hg(II) ions was monitored (Fig. 3 a). The outcomes revealed that with increasing the concentration of Hg(II) ions, the fluorescence intensity of YQDs was quenched dramatically, while the fluorescence intensity of BCDs remained almost unchanged. Fig. 3b displays the calibration plot of the ratio of the fluorescence intensities ((I0-I)/I0, I = I580/I443) against the concentration of Hg(II) ions. The signal of (I0-I)/I0 was proportional toward Hg(II) concentration from 10 nmol L−1 to 1.4 μmol L−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9924 and equation Y = 0.08209 + 0.5393X. In addition, the limit of detection (3σ/s, σ and s are the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements and the slope of the calibration curve, respectively) for Hg(II) ions was calculated to be 4.6 nmol L−1 which is lower than the allowable level of mercury (10 nmol L−1) in drinking water set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [67].

A comparison of present method with other ratiometric fluorescent sensors for determination of mercury is provided in Table S1. Among these reports, sensor systems generally consisted of sole synthetic organic dyes which always suffer from narrow excitation profile, low quantum yield and photobleaching [18]. However, we used QDs which have high quantum efficiency, broad excitation spectra, high photobleaching threshold, and narrow/symmetric band emission [5]. Besides, the present work provides the lowest reported detection limit of mercury by a ratiometric sensor (see Table S1).

To study the selectivity of the ratiometric probe, the fluorescence response of the probe was investigated towards various metal ions. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the presence of other background metal ions hardly affects the ratiometric signal compared with that obtained for Hg(II) ions. The effect of coexisted metal ions was also examined under a UV lamp. Fig. 4b illustrates that remarkable fluorescence color change occurs in the presence of Hg(II) ions and negligible changes occured after adding other metal ions to the probe.

Due to its excellent selectivity for Hg (II) detection, the proposed ratiometric probe was assessed to detect Hg(II) in real water samples (i.e., tap water, Zayanderud water, Chalus river water, Sea of Oman water and Persian Gulf water). As summerized in Table 1 , the recovery values were in the range of 97.0% to 105.3% with relative standard deviations (RSD) below 2% for each sample. The results indicated great feasibility and applicability of developed method for determination of Hg(II) in environmental samples.

In summary, we have developed a novel strategy to expand color-tunability in ratiometric fluorescent probes by simply mixing BCDs (as the internal standard) and YQDs (as the sensing fluorophore). The idea was based on the fact that the yellow fluorescence of QDs can be quenched and redshifted by the analyte, while the blue fluorescence of CDs remains intact. We have demonstrated a successful example of our developed system for Hg(II) detection in which the yellow emission of QDs was specifically quenched and redshifted upon exposure to the Hg(II), which resulted in a wide-color-varying fluorescent. Under optimized condition, the ratiometric probe demonstrated a good linear behavior, acceptable accuracy, suitable detection limit as low as 4.6 nmol L−1 with no interruption from the possible studied intereferents. It was also found that such a probe can be used for efficient detection of Hg(II) ions in complex environmental samples. We believe the principle of the proposed strategy could also be generalized to other analytes which cause redshift in the probe.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.141.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Ghasemi and Dr. Bigdeli are postdoctoral fellows at Dr. Hormozi-Nezhad’s research lab, exploring optical properties of various types of NPs. They employ the interactions of luminescent and plasmonic NPs with important (bio)markers for environmental, medicinal and point-of-care purposes.

Dr. M. Reza Hormozi-Nezhad is an Associate Professor of Analytical Chemistry at Chemistry Department and Institute of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (INST), Sharif University of Technology. His research interest mainly includes sensing and biosensing based on plasmonic and luminescent nanoparticles (NPs), shape-selective synthesis of various metallic NPs and investigating surface plasmon properties of gold and silver NPs, together with the optical properties of quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs), for the determination and discrimination of important chemical and biological active compounds.

Dr. Morteza Mahmoudi is Instructor in Anaesthesia at Brigham and Women's Hospital. He received his PhD from Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology at Sharif University of Technology (in collaboration with UC Davis, MIT, UBC, and EPFL) with specialization on the biomedical applications of superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles. He is Director of NanoBio Interaction Laboratory at Tehran University of Medical Sciences. His current research involves control of protein corona decoration at the surface of nanoparticles and hidden parameters that affect the nanobio interfaces. He was a visiting professor at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign working on detection of nanoparticles using optoelectronic sensor arrays.
